items.mistake: Rebasing a branch that others have already pulled.
items.fix: Rebase only your private branches. For shared branches, use merge (or coordinate a rebase with the whole team).
items.why: Rebase rewrites SHAs; collaborators' clones still reference the old SHAs and end up with duplicate or 'lost' commits.
items.example:
```bash
# DANGER: feature/auth is shared and has been pulled by 3 teammates.
git rebase main && git push --force   # they'll all have broken history now
# SAFE: merge instead.
git merge main
```

items.mistake: Resolving merge conflicts by deleting `<<<<<<<` markers without thinking.
items.fix: Read both sides, understand what each tried to do, write the correct combined result, then remove markers.
items.why: Conflict markers exist because Git *couldn't* decide; auto-removing them leaves whichever line happened to land in the editor.
items.example:
```
<<<<<<< HEAD
    timeout = 30
=======
    timeout = 60
>>>>>>> feature/slow-llm
# Pick deliberately — maybe even keep both for different code paths.
```

items.fix: Always `git push --force-with-lease`. It refuses if the remote moved since your last fetch.

items.mistake: Merging `main` into your feature branch over and over.
items.fix: Either rebase onto `main` (linear), or merge `main` in *once* before opening the PR.
items.why: Repeated `Merge main into feature` commits clutter history and confuse reviewers.
items.example:
```bash
# Preferred:
git rebase origin/main
# OR once:
git merge origin/main   # only when ready to PR
```

items.fix: Use `git pull --ff-only` so it refuses to make a surprise merge commit; rebase explicitly when needed.
items.why: Default `git pull` can create surprise merge commits if your local branch has diverged.
items.example:
```bash
git config --global pull.ff only   # set once
# Or inline: git pull --ff-only
```
